title: LastPass Users Had Their Data Stolen—Again
gist: This week-in-security roundup shows LastPass suffered yet another breach via a third-party AI vendor (Klue), Anthropic and the White House sparred over Claude access restrictions, Europol/Microsoft dismantled a major infostealer network, and Australia confirmed nation-state hackers pre-positioned inside critical infrastructure for potential sabotage.

themes.theme: LastPass supply-chain breach via AI vendor

insights.claim: LastPass suffered a new data breach exposing names, phone numbers, email addresses, physical addresses, support case data, and sales data, caused not by its own infrastructure but by a breach at AI business intelligence firm Klue.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that third-party AI vendors are becoming a primary breach vector even for security-focused companies — a supply-chain risk investors should weight when assessing any vendor's AI-tool integrations.
insights.quote: The attack was the result of a breach at the AI business intelligence firm Klue. Attackers compromised access tokens for Klue customers, including LastPass, and then used them to grab data from Salesforce and other integrated platforms.

insights.claim: LastPass emphasized the breach did not affect password vaults and was not a breach of its own infrastructure, framing it purely as a partner/vendor compromise.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: The vault-integrity claim is the key variable for assessing real customer risk versus reputational damage from repeated breach headlines.

insights.claim: This marks a continuation of a multi-year pattern of significant LastPass data breaches, reinforcing a track record of recurring security failures at the company.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: A pattern of repeat breaches at a password manager is a reputational and competitive-risk signal for enterprise customers evaluating password-management vendors.
insights.quote: The password manager LastPass has had a string of significant data breaches over the years, and now there's one more to add to the list.

insights.claim: Anthropic and the White House were negotiating access terms for Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models, with critics arguing Anthropic is rapidly accumulating power even as the company frames this as necessary for AI safety.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Signals an emerging political fault line over AI model access/restriction that could affect Anthropic's government and enterprise contract pipeline.
insights.quote: As Anthropic and the White House continued to negotiate a path for its latest Claude Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models, the company's critics pointed out that Anthropic seems to be rapidly accumulating power

insights.claim: The White House gave Anthropic permission to make Mythos 5 available again to a select group of US companies and government agencies on a Friday evening, implying the model had been restricted beforehand.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: A concrete resolution point in the access dispute — worth tracking whether this restricted rollout pattern recurs with future Anthropic model releases.
insights.quote: On Friday evening, the White House gave Anthropic permission to make Mythos 5 available again to a select group of US companies and government agencies.

insights.claim: OpenAI launched an improved limited-release GPT-5.5-Cyber model alongside a 'Patch the Planet' initiative to help open source projects with vulnerability patching, explicitly citing that AI is accelerating both bug discovery and exploit development.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Direct evidence that frontier AI labs now see offensive/defensive cyber capability as urgent enough to warrant dedicated model releases and initiatives — a fast-growing AI-security product category.
insights.quote: OpenAI this week launched an improved version of its limited-release GPT-5.5-Cyber model as well as a full-scale effort—"Patch the Planet"—to support open source projects on vulnerability patching and other security issues as AI accelerates bug discovery as well as exploit development.

insights.claim: WIRED reporting found that both US and Chinese AI experts are separately worried about the AI arms race reaching a 'Chernobyl moment.'
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: A shared catastrophic-risk framing across geopolitical rivals suggests genuine cross-border alignment on AI safety concerns, distinct from purely competitive rhetoric.
insights.quote: as the AI arms race between China and the US escalates, WIRED met with a slew of China's top AI experts and found that both sides are worried about the threat of a "Chernobyl moment."

insights.claim: Microsoft, Europol and partners disrupted the Amadey and StealC infostealer infrastructure as part of Operation Endgame, seizing 326 servers and 142 domains, flagging about $47 million in stolen cryptocurrency and recovering up to 27 million stolen access credentials.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of criminal infostealer infrastructure (27 million credentials) that had been circulating before takedown — informs risk models for credential-stuffing attacks.
insights.quote: The action involved identifying, mapping, and then seizing and taking down malware infrastructure, including actions against 326 servers and 142 domains.

insights.claim: Microsoft said the takedown was enabled by AI-assisted analysis that revealed Amadey and StealC were relying on the same backend infrastructure, allowing both to be targeted together.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: A concrete case of AI tooling directly enabling a major law-enforcement cybercrime takedown — evidence of AI's defensive-side ROI in security operations.
insights.quote: Microsoft emphasized that the action was enabled by innovative techniques including AI-assisted analysis that showed Amadey and StealC were relying on the same backend infrastructure and could be targeted together.

insights.claim: Australia's intelligence agency ASIO said it discovered nation-state hackers already inside a critical infrastructure provider's network, assessed to be preparing for sabotage by mapping the network and maintaining access, including acquiring credentials of the IT staff guarding it.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Direct evidence of pre-positioned nation-state sabotage capability inside critical infrastructure — a live geopolitical/cyber-risk signal for infrastructure operators and their insurers globally.
insights.quote: We discovered nation-state hackers had compromised the network of an Australian critical infrastructure provider. ASIO assessed the hackers were preparing for sabotage. … They were mapping out the network and maintaining access so they could cripple it at a time of their choosing.
insights.locator: ASIO director general Mike Burgess quote
